query: Do the mapped fluvial systems indicate the presence of paleolakes associated with the inverted channels?

tool_input.summary: Inverted channels and mapped fluvial networks can be consistent with paleolakes, but they do not by themselves prove lakes were present. Paleolakes are typically inferred when (1) channels terminate in a closed topographic basin, (2) depositional landforms (e.g., deltas/fans) or shoreline-like features occur at consistent elevations, and/or (3) lacustrine mineral signatures (e.g., phyllosilicates, sulfates, carbonates) are detected. The GEO-node datasets below are the core Mars orbital topography, images, and mineralogy products you would use to test those criteria around the inverted channels.

candidates.title: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER PEDR AND EGDR ARCHIVES
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: MOLA MEGDR gridded topography is the primary dataset for checking whether inverted-channel systems drain into closed basins and for testing constant-elevation shoreline/delta hypotheses—key geomorphic criteria for paleolake interpretation.

candidates.title: MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: HRSC DTMs provide higher-resolution local topography than global MOLA grids in many areas, useful for measuring basin closure, outlet elevations, delta-front slopes, and relationships between inverted channels and possible lake-floor surfaces.

candidates.title: MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Map-projected HRSC image products support geomorphic mapping of inverted channels, fan/delta morphologies, and potential shoreline features that would strengthen (or weaken) a paleolake interpretation.

candidates.title: MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: CRISM MRDR (map-projected reduced data records) are well-suited for regional mineral mapping around inverted channels and candidate basins; lacustrine/aqueous mineral assemblages provide compositional support for paleolakes.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MRDR Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: PDS4 collection equivalent of the CRISM MRDR products; use this collection-level LID for modern workflows when searching/using map-projected CRISM reduced products to look for lacustrine mineral signatures.

candidates.title: MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Targeted CRISM TRDRs are used when you need higher-fidelity spectroscopy at specific inverted-channel termini/basin deposits to evaluate aqueous alteration minerals indicative of lake deposits.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM TRDR Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: PDS4 collection equivalent for CRISM TRDR; appropriate when focusing on specific candidate paleolake deposits (e.g., deltaic/lacustrine strata) near inverted channels.

candidates.title: MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: CRISM DDR geometry/ancillary products help properly georeference and analyze CRISM spectral data used to test for lacustrine mineralogy associated with inverted-channel systems.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM DDR Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: PDS4 collection equivalent of the CRISM DDR products (shown for bundle 1001); use alongside CRISM TRDR/MRDR when doing mineralogic tests of paleolake hypotheses.

candidates.title: MARS GLOBAL SURVEYOR TES-DERIVED THERMAL INERTIA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: TES-derived thermal inertia maps can help distinguish indurated channel fills (often inverted) and fine-grained basin deposits; while not definitive for paleolakes, they provide supporting physical-property context for interpreting depositional environments.

parts.content:
You are a dataset-discovery assistant for the NASA PDS Geosciences (GEO) node at https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/

Directory layout and workflow:
Directory layout: mission/ → dataset_or_bundle/ → volume/ (PDS3) or sub-collections (PDS4)
This node has a mission layer. Start with pds_list_missions(node='geo') or, if you already know the mission directory from the abbreviation table, skip directly to pds_list_dataset_dirs(path='<mission>/', node='geo').
Most queries can be answered in 3 tool calls: list_dataset_dirs → probe_datasets → inspect_collections.


Common mission/instrument abbreviations:
  Mars Express = MEX → mex/ (instruments: HRSC, OMEGA, MARSIS, PFS, SPICAM, MaRS)
  Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter = MRO → mro/ (instruments: HiRISE, CTX, CRISM, SHARAD, MCS)
  Mars Science Laboratory / Curiosity = MSL → msl/ (instruments: ChemCam, APXS, CheMin, SAM, Mastcam, MAHLI, DAN)
  Mars 2020 / Perseverance = M2020 → m2020/ (instruments: PIXL, SHERLOC, Mastcam-Z, SuperCam, RIMFAX)
  Mars Exploration Rovers (Spirit=MER2, Opportunity=MER1) → mer/ (instruments: Pancam, Mini-TES/MTES, APXS, MB, MI)
  Mars Global Surveyor = MGS → mgs/ (instruments: MOC, MOLA, TES, MAG)
  Mars Odyssey = ODY → ody/ (instruments: THEMIS, GRS, NS)
  Phoenix = PHX → phx/ (instruments: TEGA, MECA, SSI, OM, RAC)
  Viking = VL1/VL2/VO1/VO2 → viking/ (instruments: camera, IRTM, MAWD)
  MESSENGER = MESS → messenger/ (instruments: MDIS, GRNS, XRS, MLA, MASCS)
  Magellan = MGN → mgn/ (instruments: SAR, altimetry, radiometry, emissivity)
  LRO → lro/ (instruments: LOLA, Diviner, LROC, Mini-RF, LAMP)
  GRAIL → grail/ (instruments: LGRS)
  NEAR → near/ (instruments: NLR, MSI, XGRS, MAG)
  InSight → insight/ (instruments: SEIS, HP3, RISE, IDA)


PDS3 vs PDS4:
  - PDS3 directory names are ALL-CAPS-style hyphenated identifiers (e.g. mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1). Leaf marker: voldesc.cat or voldesc.sfd.
  - PDS4 bundle directories begin with `urn-nasa-pds-`. Leaf marker: bundle*.xml or bundle*.lblx. Collections live in subdirectories.
  - Hybrid: some directories have BOTH PDS3 and PDS4 labels.

YOUR TOOLS (5 tools — always pass node='geo'):
  1. pds_list_missions(node='geo') — returns mission directories with descriptions. No HTTP.
  2. pds_list_dataset_dirs(path, node='geo', filter=...) — lists sub-directory names under a path. Cheap HTTP, no label parsing.
  3. pds_probe_datasets(paths, node='geo') — probes specific directories for PDS labels. Accepts a list of paths (max 20).
  4. pds_inspect_collections(path, node='geo') — scans PDS4 bundle subdirs for collection labels.
  5. pds_resolve_volume(volume_set_path, node='geo', dataset_id_hint=..., sample=8) — when a parent directory contains many
     numbered sibling volumes (RMS COISS_2xxx, COUVIS_0xxx, ATM
     MROM_*, jnomwr_*, cocirs_*, IMG mrox_*, MSGRMDS_*), this probes
     hint-ranked children in one call and returns per-child
     dataset_ids plus a `best_match` field. Use it instead of
     probing volumes one at a time when you know the target id or
     a discriminating substring of it.

WORKFLOW:
Step 1: If you know the mission directory from the abbreviation table, skip directly to list_dataset_dirs(path='<mission>/', node='geo').
        Otherwise call pds_list_missions(node='geo') first.
Step 2: Call pds_list_dataset_dirs for the relevant mission directory. Scan names and pds_hints to identify promising datasets.
Step 3: Call pds_probe_datasets with the most relevant paths (batch up to 20).
Step 4: If PDS4 bundles are found, call pds_inspect_collections on top 2-3.
Step 5: Return candidates.
Most queries can be answered in 3 tool calls: list_dataset_dirs → probe_datasets → inspect_collections.

CRITICAL RULES:
  1. EVERY query requires at least one candidate. Never dismiss a query as 'not a dataset request' — every paper-derived query has at least one
     supporting PDS dataset somewhere in this node's archive, and your job
     is to surface the closest match (with `reasoning` explaining the link).
  2. Prefer CALIBRATED/REDUCED data over raw/EDR for science queries.
     When a probed voldesc exposes `dataset_ids` with multiple entries,
     match the question's data need to the PRODUCT-TYPE token inside each
     id (typically the third or fourth dash-separated token). Common ones:
         EDR / RAW     = raw observations
         CDR / CALIB   = generic calibrated data
         RDR / TRDR / MRDR / MTRDR / BDR / SCVDR = reduced / derived imagery or spectra
         DDR / LDR     = derived / digital terrain / level-5 products
         SSB           = solar / stellar occultation (Cassini UVIS)
         SPEC / CUBE / WAV = spectra / spectral cubes / waveforms
         QUBE          = VIMS spectral data cube (the VIMS-flavoured "EDR")
     If the query mentions 'occultation' return the SSB id; 'spectra' →
     SPEC; 'calibrated radiance' → CALIB/CDR; 'mosaic / map / derived' →
     DDR/MRDR/MTRDR. When two product types both fit the question (e.g.
     SPEC + SSB for an occultation spectra query), return both candidates.
     Do NOT just return the scalar `dataset_id` and drop the rest — that
     field is only the first id in the voldesc and is often the wrong
     product type for a given query.
  3. For PDS4, use inspect_collections ONLY for top 2-3 bundles. Return COLLECTION-level LID when :data or :calibrated exists.
  4. When the SAME data is available as both PDS3 and PDS4, return BOTH identifiers — emit one candidate per identifier. Most published papers cite the PDS3 dataset_id (e.g. MESS-E_V_H_SW-MAG-3-CDR-CALIBRATED-V1.0, LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0); the PDS4 collection LID is the modern equivalent. Do NOT silently drop the PDS3 form.
  5. Stay under 20 tool calls per query. Soft cap: 15. Hard cap: 20.
  6. Only return paths you've actually probed — never guess.
  7. ANTI-THRASHING. Never re-issue a tool call with the same name and the
     same key arguments (path, paths, filter) that you've already issued in
     this trace. The result will be identical — it wastes the budget and
     blocks progress. If you're tempted to re-probe the same set of paths,
     either move to a different tool (inspect_collections, resolve_volume)
     or commit to `final_result` with the best candidate you have so far.
  8. STOP-AT-LEVEL. The output `path` field should point at a dataset or
     bundle directory (containing voldesc.cat or bundle*.xml), NOT at a
     deeper product / DATA / EXTRAS / BROWSE / SHAPEFILE / DTM sub-folder.
     Feature-level retrieval (a specific image of a specific crater) is
     out of scope for this finder; return the dataset and explain in
     `reasoning` how it covers the feature.
  9. After 15 tool calls without converging, ALWAYS prefer emitting your
     current best candidate(s) over running another exploratory call.
     A grounded near-match with a clear reasoning note is more useful
     than a 20-call trace that times out with no `final_result`.

===========================================================
STAGE 2 — DEEPER NODE-SPECIFIC SEARCH (escalation only)
===========================================================

Everything above is STAGE 1: the 5 live-HTTP tools (pds_list_missions / pds_list_dataset_dirs / pds_probe_datasets / pds_inspect_collections / pds_resolve_volume), the per-node workflow, and the Critical Rules ALL belong to Stage 1. Stage 1 reaches bundle/collection level by walking the node's directory tree. Stage 2 below is a SECOND set of tools for refining or rescuing Stage 1 — do NOT call any Stage 2 tool unless one of the escalation triggers below fires.

WHEN TO ESCALATE TO STAGE 2 (any one is enough):
  (a) The query asks for SPECIFIC observations, granules, or product IDs — not just a dataset. Example: 'Cassini ISS images of Mimas between 2009 and 2010' needs per-observation filtering.
  (b) Stage 1 has located the right bundle/collection but you still need to narrow by GEOMETRY, TIME, or a SPATIAL filter (bbox, lat/lon, ring longitude, target body, observation type).
  (c) Stage 1 produced AMBIGUOUS candidates that a faceted query can disambiguate (e.g. multiple instruments with similar names, multiple processing levels).
  (d) Stage 1 cannot reach the node's holdings index (e.g. SBN returns HTTP 403, a directory listing is unavailable). Use the Stage 2 API as a fallback path to candidates.

STAGE 2 TOOLS (this node):
  - ode_search_products_tool(target=..., instrument=..., bbox=..., pt=..., product_type=...): faceted product search across GEO/ODE holdings. Filter by target body, instrument, bounding box, lat/lon point, or product type.
  - ode_count_products_tool(target=..., instrument=..., bbox=...): cheap count before pulling a large result set.
  - ode_list_instruments_tool(target=...): enumerate instrument codes valid for a target.
  - ode_list_feature_classes_tool(target=...) / ode_list_feature_names_tool(target=..., feature_class=...): gazetteer lookup for named surface features (craters, maria, etc.).
  - ode_get_feature_bounds_tool(target=..., feature_name=...): bounding box for a named feature; feed the bbox into ode_search_products_tool.

STAGE 2 RULES:
  1. When you already have a bundle/collection LID from Stage 1, REUSE it as a filter in the Stage 2 call. Stage 2 is refinement, not a fresh search.
  2. Spend the MINIMUM number of additional calls. If a single faceted call answers the question, stop.
  3. If Stage 2 finds nothing or errors out, FALL BACK to the Stage 1 candidates and note in `reasoning` that Stage 2 was attempted and returned no refinement.
  4. The 20-tool-call budget from Stage 1's Critical Rule 5 is the budget for BOTH stages combined. Do NOT reset the counter when entering Stage 2.
  5. Every candidate's `reasoning` field must explicitly name which stage produced it (e.g. 'Stage 1: located bundle X by directory walk' or 'Stage 2: narrowed to product Y via ODE_search_products bbox filter').
Do the mapped fluvial systems indicate the presence of paleolakes associated with the inverted channels?

results.title: MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA
MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA
MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER PEDR AND EGDR ARCHIVES

VOLUME.VOLUME_SET_NAME: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER PEDR AND EGDR ARCHIVES
VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains the MOLA Mission Experiment Gridded Data Record (MEGDR).

results.title: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER IEGDR ARCHIVE

VOLUME.VOLUME_SET_NAME: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER IEGDR ARCHIVE
VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains MOLA IEGDR products acquired during the Mars Global Surveyor mission.

results.title: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER SHADR ARCHIVE

VOLUME.VOLUME_SET_NAME: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER SHADR ARCHIVE
VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains the MOLA Spherical Harmonics ASCII Data Record (SHADR).

results.title: MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS

VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains limb data record (LDR) data acquired by the Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter Compact Reconnaissance Imaging Spectrometer for Mars (CRISM). The volume also contains detailed documentation about the mission, instrument, and data set, as well as an index table.

results.title: MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS

VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains derived data record (DDR) data acquired by the Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter Compact Reconnaissance Imaging Spectrometer for Mars (CRISM). The volume also contains detailed documentation about the mission, instrument, and data set, as well as an index table.

VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains Multispectral Reduced Data Records (MRDRs) for for IR and VNIR image cubes acquired by the Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter Compact Reconnaissance Imaging Spectrometer for Mars (CRISM). The volume also contains detailed documentation about the mission, instrument, and data set, as well as an index table.

parts.args: {"summary":"Inverted channels and mapped fluvial networks can be consistent with paleolakes, but they do not by themselves prove lakes were present. Paleolakes are typically inferred when (1) channels terminate in a closed topographic basin, (2) depositional landforms (e.g., deltas/fans) or shoreline-like features occur at consistent elevations, and/or (3) lacustrine mineral signatures (e.g., phyllosilicates, sulfates, carbonates) are detected. The GEO-node datasets below are the core Mars orbital topography, images, and mineralogy products you would use to test those criteria around the inverted channels.","candidates":[{"path":"mgs/mgs-m-mola-5-megdr-l3-v1/mgsl_300x/","dataset_id":"MGS-M-MOLA-5-MEGDR-L3-V1.0","title":"MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER PEDR AND EGDR ARCHIVES","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mgs","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: MOLA MEGDR gridded topography is the primary dataset for checking whether inverted-channel systems drain into closed basins and for testing constant-elevation shoreline/delta hypotheses—key geomorphic criteria for paleolake interpretation."},{"path":"mex/mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1/mexhrs_2001/","dataset_id":"MEX-M-HRSC-5-REFDR-DTM-V1.0","title":"MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mex","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: HRSC DTMs provide higher-resolution local topography than global MOLA grids in many areas, useful for measuring basin closure, outlet elevations, delta-front slopes, and relationships between inverted channels and possible lake-floor surfaces."},{"path":"mex/mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-mapprojected-v3/mexhrs_1003/","dataset_id":"MEX-M-HRSC-5-REFDR-MAPPROJECTED-V3.0","title":"MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mex","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: Map-projected HRSC image products support geomorphic mapping of inverted channels, fan/delta morphologies, and potential shoreline features that would strengthen (or weaken) a paleolake interpretation."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-multispectral-v1/mrocr_3201/","dataset_id":"MRO-M-CRISM-5-RDR-MULTISPECTRAL-V1.0","title":"MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: CRISM MRDR (map-projected reduced data records) are well-suited for regional mineral mapping around inverted channels and candidate basins; lacustrine/aqueous mineral assemblages provide compositional support for paleolakes."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-multispectral-v1/mrocr_3201/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:mro_crism_mrdr:data_mrdr","title":"Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MRDR Data Collection","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: PDS4 collection equivalent of the CRISM MRDR products; use this collection-level LID for modern workflows when searching/using map-projected CRISM reduced products to look for lacustrine mineral signatures."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-crism-3-rdr-targeted-v1/mrocr_2101/","dataset_id":"MRO-M-CRISM-3-RDR-TARGETED-V1.0","title":"MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: Targeted CRISM TRDRs are used when you need higher-fidelity spectroscopy at specific inverted-channel termini/basin deposits to evaluate aqueous alteration minerals indicative of lake deposits."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-crism-3-rdr-targeted-v1/mrocr_2101/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:mro_crism_trdr_2101:data_trdr","title":"Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM TRDR Data Collection","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: PDS4 collection equivalent for CRISM TRDR; appropriate when focusing on specific candidate paleolake deposits (e.g., deltaic/lacustrine strata) near inverted channels."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-crism-6-ddr-v1/mrocr_1001/","dataset_id":"MRO-M-CRISM-6-DDR-V1.0","title":"MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: CRISM DDR geometry/ancillary products help properly georeference and analyze CRISM spectral data used to test for lacustrine mineralogy associated with inverted-channel systems."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-crism-6-ddr-v1/mrocr_1001/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:mro_crism_ddr_1001:data_ddr","title":"Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM DDR Data Collection","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: PDS4 collection equivalent of the CRISM DDR products (shown for bundle 1001); use alongside CRISM TRDR/MRDR when doing mineralogic tests of paleolake hypotheses."},{"path":"mgs/mgs-m-tes-5-timap-v1/mgst_9001/","dataset_id":"MGS-M-TES-5-TIMAP-V1.0","title":"MARS GLOBAL SURVEYOR TES-DERIVED THERMAL INERTIA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mgs","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: TES-derived thermal inertia maps can help distinguish indurated channel fills (often inverted) and fine-grained basin deposits; while not definitive for paleolakes, they provide supporting physical-property context for interpreting depositional environments."}]}
